Object 5.title: Object 5
Object 5.description:
Object 5, nicknamed by wanderers as Greek Fire, is an essential resource found within the Backrooms and is typically the first weapon one would procure during their journey through the Main Nine. Object 5 takes the appearance of a metallic clipped box, similar to the outlook of a first-aid kit stripped of any label. The box contains an ordinary plastic lighter identical to those in the Frontrooms, and an unmarked green aluminium bottle lying on a foam insert. The lighter works as any ordinary lighter would, while the bottle contains a dark-yellow fluid with empirical statistics similar to that of vegetable oil.

The fluid in question is a highly volatile, flammable substance that can be used to start fires, heat food, and provide much-needed warmth inside the Backrooms. The fluid was nicknamed Object 5.3, while the bottle was nicknamed Object 5.4. The anomalous properties of Object 5 presents itself when the fluid is shaken inside of its original container. When violently agitated inside of its container for approximately 3 seconds, Object 5.3 gains universal oleophobicity. This indicates that all surfaces will repel it, similar to the behaviour of water on super-hydrophobic surfaces. The fluid will become nigh-frictionless with almost any medium it flows across, creating a mesmerising flowing effect. This new liquid was deemed as Object 5.31.

While oleophobicity is not anomalous by itself, universal oleophobicity is an enigmatic behaviour that cannot be purely explained via diffusion through agitation. Combined with its combustive nature, the lighter transforms Object 5 into a deadly incendiary weapon. Lighting the bottle at its neck while pouring its contents out will send a steady stream of raging fire towards any desired target. Greek Fire cannot be extinguished via conventional means, because the inhibitor will simply slide off the puddle of Object 5.31 and the flame will continue to burn. Greek Fire can result in fatal burns and considerable damage to its target, may it be an entity, structure, or human enemy. Object 5's striking similarity to a napalm weapon dated back in the Eastern Roman Empire known as Greek Fire earned it the fitting name Greek Fire.

However, it is important to note that this oleophobic behaviour will begin to slowly fade after 10 minutes, until the anomaly disappearance all together in 30 minutes. Combined with the observation that this effect only takes place when the fluid is within its original container, it is widely believed that there is a coating on the inside of Object 5.4. The shaking induces a catalyst that results in some form of molecular alteration. This does not explain how the effect dissipates after a set amount of time, since chemical changes are hard to reverse. As of now, there is no equipment powerful enough to confirm nor deny this theory. The anomaly of Object 5 lies in the shadows.

Object 33.title: Object 33
Object 33.description:
Object 33: "Memory Flame"

Description

Memory Flame, also referred to as "Burning Memories," is a highly radioactive black colored element with the molecular symbol Me. It is well recognized for being extremely dangerous and erasing the user's memory, but it can also, very rarely, result in the user's death. Being near the Memory Flame is not advised due to its extreme risk, as even the smell of it can seriously harm one's lungs and in some cases result in death. As such, it should be approached only when it is absolutely necessary. While touching the Memory Flame doesn't usually cause memory loss, it leads to death.

While it is usually found in nature as a liquid, it can occasionally be found as a solid or even as a gas. Memory Flame can pollute the air in a place and seriously harm people's lungs if it stays in an area for a long amount of time. Researchers are still studying Memory Flame and attempting to duplicate Memorestoration, which opposes its effects.

Keep in mind that these effects are irreversible and cannot be undone in any way aside of using Memorestoration. It is rare to find Memorestoration in nature, therefore if someone is affected by Memory Flame, there should always be someone who can find Memorestoration for them.

Memory Flame should never be used on entities, as it makes them more aggressive rather than incapacitating them. Strangely, however, Hounds have been seen to run away from Memory Flame.

Effects

The user will experience some changes as a result of use over time.

Origin

Jerry M. Sercher and a few B.W.O. members first discovered Memory Flame at Level 2 in 1992 when they noticed it leaking from a pipe. There is no information available about Memory Flame's origins.

Locations

Although Memory Flame can be found in many levels of the Backrooms, it is most commonly found in Level 2. This liquid appears to flow through the pipes of Level 2.

Compounds-Variants

MeO: Red Flame
Mixing Memory Flame and Oxygen will result in a variant of Memory Flame, which is Red Flame. Its main difference from the Memory Flame is that Red Flame instantly kills the user instead of erasing their memory. Another difference is that it's color is red instead of black.

MeCt2: Memorestoration
Mixing Memory Flame and Corruptium will produce Memorestoration.

Object 17.title: Object 17
Object 17.description:
The Lubik's Cube, designated as Object 17, resembles a Rubik's Cube with minor differences, like mild difficulty turning the pieces, slightly washed-out colors, and various anomalous properties that may even determine the fate of those who toy with it. Once a wanderer touches a Lubik's Cube, its colors will wash out, the cube will turn cold despite the surrounding temperature, and the cube's pieces can no longer be turned by other wanderers. The cube can teleport to any nearby position at random, but usually does so when other wanderers force the pieces once another wanderer already "owns" it or when wanderers try to damage the cube. If the owner has been solving the cube for at least one minute straight, the cube's colors may randomly shift into different positions, usually reversing all solving progress.

The wanderer who "owns" the Lubik's Cube will be unable to leave their current level and will be strongly compelled to solve the cube, often giving it their complete focus and ignoring other errands. Owners who encounter any complication regarding the cube, such as the cube teleporting, the cube's colors switching positions, or trying to find solutions to solve it with no avail, will often incur a short burst of anger. The outrage may lead to the user damaging the cube, and when it sustains enough damage, it may deliver detrimental or even fatal outcomes for the owner.

After thirty to sixty minutes after a wanderer claims the Lubik's Cube, it will suddenly vanish, and the user will be able to exit their current level. Strangely, their chances of encountering and interacting with another Lubik's Cube increase, with their subsequent cube encounter within 2-24 months. Knowing about the cube also slightly increases one's chances of encountering it, with roughly 60-80% of Lubik's Cubes encountered by those who knew it beforehand, with many seeing a potential link to the Kauer Thesis. So far, since its discovery in 1982, less than a thousand cubes have been discovered, with the rate of manifestation increasing over time.

Once the owner solves one side of the Lubik's Cube, it will assign a random outcome. The color of the solved side determines the ramification and its magnitude. When the "owner" solves one side and meets their fate, the Cube will disappear and the "owner" will not encounter another cube for 45-75 days. The cube then vanishes shortly after the fate has been granted. If two sides are solved, the wanderer will experience two fates simultaneously, one from each color, if feasible. In the extremely rare scenario where someone manages to solve a side on a Lubik's Cube without ever having touched it (example being with chopsticks), the cube will simply scramble itself again, and will not provide an outcome unless a wanderer touches, and "owns" it before solving a side. Some colors have fatal ramifications, including ones where the user's death will be of suffering. Inversely, some outcomes may grant bountiful blessings, incentivizing wanderers to play with the cube despite the cost.

Fortunately, listed below are the consequences of each color, so one may be cautious when encountering and solving a Lubik's Cube.
Object 17.lead:
I was in Level 213 with a friend of mine who had returned from a brief expedition. He went through a few levels, expecting to find something interesting or even just a single can of Almond Water or a growth of Backshrooms. However, all he found was this Rubik's Cube, which he found on his way to our meetup point in the level. It did pique his interest a bit since he's been in the Backrooms for 3 years now and this is the first time he saw a Rubik's Cube in these boundless expanses, and he was happy that he found something which he can toy around even if he had no clue how to solve it.

Before he would start playing with it though, he wanted to show it to me and give him some advice on how to solve it. We both grabbed a quick snack from the diner, and we sat down at this table where he would toy with the Rubiks Cube and talk about it with me. He would say about how he's finally excited to have some form of entertainment in the Backrooms, but knowing he had no idea how to solve it, I couldn't see such expression on his face. He continued to toy with it further, leaving his meal untouched, with mine almost finished.

He was almost getting one side of the Rubiks Cube finished, and he was happy about it. I found that neat, but I still had to focus on finishing my meal. I looked to my plate and as I was getting the last bouts of food I had left, I heard a massive bang-like sound, and my surroundings were splattered with blood all over. I looked up, and I saw a horrifying scene which I'll never forget - it seems like the Rubiks Cube hit my friend in the face with the force of a bullet. It left a gory scene leaving my poor pal unrecognizable, and I could see deep in the face the Rubik's Cube - deformed and wiggling a bit, like it had a sentience on it's own. It then disappeared, leaving a hole on the other side of the head.

----

Object 50.title: Object 50
Object 50.description:
Object 50, scientifically referred to as Capillum Aethertemptor, are thick strands which imitate the texture and appearance of human hair. Their location varies from level to level, they can be observed hanging from the ceiling, no-clipping through walls, or seemingly floating mid-air. Capillum Aethertemptor typically inhabit dimly lit and uninhabited areas, however they can uncommonly be found within other areas as well.

Capillum Aethertemptor do not appear naturally within the Backrooms' levels, instead they are manually placed by entities. Entities utilize Capillum Aethertemptor to lure wanderers, they specifically use strands resembling hair to intrigue their prey as hair isn't commonly found within the Backrooms. Capillum Aethertemptor emit a ginormous glow, making wanderers believe that they have stumbled upon an exit. The location of Capillum Aethertemptor depends on the intelligence of the entities that place them. Entities which use Capillum Aethertemptor the most are: Skin-Stealers, Facelings and Clumps. Usage of Capillum Aethertemptor is also utilized when determining an entities intelligence.

Wanderers who have encountered Capillum Aethertemptor have reported feeling a strong urge to walk towards it and touch it, and that no matter how hard they tried they could not resist the impulse. The exact cause of this is unknown, though it is hypothesized to be another anomalous property that Capillum Aethertemptor possess.

The origin of Capillum Aethertemptor is not yet determined. Some claim that entities steal human hair from wanderers, as they highly resemble real strands of hair, though others claim that they are made by the entities themselves, citing the anomalous characteristics of Capillum Aethertemptor.

Object 20.description:
----

The Cloth Phone

>> Object Classification: Mental Hazard

>> Location: Variable
Object 20.lead:
----

The Cloth Phone

>> Object Classification: Mental Hazard

>> Location: Variable

entrances.text: Noclipping through the clouds of the Cloud Plane in Level 399 may lead one here.
It is theorized there is a direct entrance from Levels 385, 386, and 387; however this is unconfirmed.

exits.text: Falling through the clouds can bring one either to The Void, Level 385, Level 386, Level 387, Level 0, or Level 399.
Riding a drifting cloud through the level may cause one to enter Level 993.1.
No-clipping in offices direct one most often to Level 4,
No-clipping in bars direct one most often to Level 610,
No-clipping in houses direct one most often to Level 201 or Level 231,
And No-clipping in stores directs one most often to Level 711.

Object 44.title: Object 44
Object 44.description:
M.E.G. Warning

We hereby issue a warning to all wanderers. It is to be noted that Cashew Water is a liquid substance that poses significant harm to the human body. Under no circumstances should any wanderer consume this liquid by drinking it or have direct physical contact with it. This is of utmost importance for the well-being and safety of all wanderers. Failure to abide by this directive may result in adverse consequences to one's health. We earnestly request all wanderers to take it upon themselves to inform those who are not yet aware of this matter.

Object 44
Cashew Water

 I.Overview
Object 44, commonly known as Cashew Water, is an object that can be encountered in the Backrooms. Cashew Water exhibits a yellowish hue and, upon initial observation, may not possess any readily apparent and distinguishable differences from Almond Water. It is habitually carried in various containers, among which glass bottles are more frequent. The M.E.G. solemnly requests that any wanderer who discovers a container holding Cashew Water should immediately affix a label to it. This action is taken to prevent potential perils and safeguard the well-being of all individuals within the Backrooms. When essential for the sake of safety, Cashew Water can be destroyed.

Cashew Water is believed to be relatively more common in unstable levels. However, it can also be found in regular levels. According to M.E.G. levelogists, this is thought to be attributed to the possibility that Cashew Water may be generated by so-called unstable matter. Unstable levels are predominantly composed of unstable matter. As a result, Cashew Water is more prevalent in these areas and is subsequently spread throughout various levels. It should be noted that this is merely one of many conjectures. Further research and exploration are required to confirm or refute this hypothesis and to gain a deeper understanding of the nature and origin of Cashew Water within the Backrooms.

Presently, in light of the currently available knowledge regarding Cashew Water, it is widely and thoroughly considered to be in direct opposition to Almond Water. When we delve into its influence on mental stability, the effect is strikingly and completely contrary. Cashew Water is capable of inducing intense sensations of fear and anxiety within an individual, leading to a significant deterioration of sanity. Eventually, those who consume Cashew Water – if they are fortunate enough to avoid a painful death – may transform into a Wretch.

Furthermore, upon conducting more in-depth examination and comprehensive analysis, it has been uncovered that some specific varieties of Cashew Water contain minuscule yet detectable amounts of liquid pain. In addition, various acids are present in Cashew Water posing a substantial and formidable threat to the human body. These acids can potentially cause detrimental effects on certain physiological systems and overall well-being, disrupting normal bodily functions and compromising one's health.

Cashew Water tends to attract a variety of entities. If you inadvertently spill Cashew Water on a certain level, it may attract large numbers of hostile entities. This property can sometimes be strategically exploited (more on this will be discussed later) and is exclusively useful in limited circumstances. Splashing Cashew Water on an entity can have a significant impact. It will increase the entity's intelligence, physical strength, and potentially other attributes. However, there is an important exception to this rule. If the entity been transformed by a human, then Cashew Water will have the same effect on such an entity as it would on a human being.

Cashew Water also has an expiration date, as do its effects. However, this does not in any way mean that they are safe to drink. Even when the negative effects are somewhat reduced, there are still significant risks associated with consuming Cashew Water, and it should be avoided at all costs.

 II.Distinguishing
Sometimes, Cashew Water is cunningly disguised as other liquids, making it extremely difficult to determine its true nature. So, is the bottle of liquid you are currently holding in your hand Cashew Water? Certainly, you must never attempt to tell if they are Cashew Water by drinking them. Not only is this extremely dangerous, but it is also unethical to give potentially harmful substances to anyone else. You'll most likely need the following methods to tell the difference:

Cashew Water has a unique property of attracting entities. If you've perused the documentation on Facelings, you'll probably be familiar with Officer Atticus N. Wolfe. In case you carry a bottle of Cashew Water, the good-natured Faceling may be drawn over due to the allure of the Cashew Water. This friendly entity might then tell you that the liquid is not drinkable. Thus, you can try to find a friendly, willing, and communicative entity and ask if the liquid in your hand is Cashew Water. However, this approach is not always guaranteed to work as not all entities may be aware of or willing to help identify Cashew Water.

Almond water holds the remarkable ability to counteract all the effects of Cashew Water. If you have a sufficient amount of almond water in your possession, it may seem feasible to drink a little of the liquid in your hand to determine if it's Cashew Water. If you find yourself in a trance and your sanity is reduced, drinking almond water immediately can potentially reverse the effects. At this point, if your condition improves, you can conclude that it was a bottle of Cashew Water. However, this method is incredibly risky. If you don't drink almond water in time, you are highly likely to die a painful and agonizing death. The stakes are extremely high, and one must think carefully before attempting such a dangerous approach.

Another way to identify Cashew Water is by smelling the smell. If you can detect a distinct aroma of cashew nuts, it could be a bottle of Cashew Water. However, it is important to note that not all Cashew Water has a cashew nut flavor. Some varieties may have a more subtle or even no discernible scent related to cashew nuts. This makes relying solely on smell a less reliable method, but it can still be a useful clue in combination with other techniques.

 III.Usage
Cashew Water is indeed not overly useful as an object that strengthens entities while simultaneously weakening humans. It presents a rather precarious balance that must be carefully considered. If you find yourself with the intention of hunting entities, which are often rare and elusive, whether it's for the purpose of making a meal or utilizing the ingredients on them, Cashew Water can be employed as a means to attract these entities. However, this is an extremely dangerous endeavor. The slightest miscalculation or misstep can result in attracting far too many entities that surround you, leaving you in a perilous situation from which escape may seem nearly impossible. You risk being torn apart by these entities, with little chance of survival.

In addition, when Cashew Water is mixed with liquid pain, an even more dangerous concoction is created. This combination can be fashioned into a time bomb of sorts. The timing of its explosion is entirely random, adding an element of unpredictability and risk. On one hand, this can be a double-edged sword. While it poses a significant threat to those who are unaware or unprepared, it can also be utilized for exploring levels.

 IV.Discovery

Once a fearless wanderer named Phoebe was engaged in an exploration of Level 1. During her journey, she came across several bottles that appeared to her as if they were bottles of almond water. Unaware of the true nature of the liquid, she consumed one of the bottles. Little did she know that it was actually a large bottle of Cashew Water. Soon after drinking it, she began to experience a sense of unease and discomfort. Struggling with great effort, she made her way towards M.E.G. Base First Light. The doctors stationed at the base promptly initiated a diagnosis. Regrettably, at that time, no one within the vicinity was familiar with the existence of Cashew Water. As a result, Phoebe found herself in an extremely painful state, and became a wretch. In the end, the M.E.G. agents had no choice but to carry out an execution.

Later on, it was unexpectedly discovered that Phoebe still had several bottles of liquid remaining in her bag. Scientists began to speculate that it was precisely the consumption of this liquid that had led to Phoebe's tragic demise. This speculation was subsequently confirmed by the doctors after a meticulous dissection of Phoebe's body. Subsequently, following an in-depth study of these liquids, its properties were gradually uncovered. Due to its distinct cashew flavor and the contrasting properties to almond water, it was ultimately given the name "Cashew Water".

Original Author: Taangeerine
Previously rewritten by: MoonlightSerenityRewritten by: EveryBox

Object 92.title: Object 92
Object 92.description:
Bottle 'o Void is a liquid substance which comes prepackaged within bottles. This substance is purple in color and has small portions of a sparkling element. Bottle 'o Void instances have been observed to only spawn within dimmer levels, and in exceedingly rare occurrences, The Void.

Bottle 'o Void is incredibly useful for construction purposes throughout the Backrooms, as it has been proven to be able to easily remove geometric matter upon contact with it. Via unknown means, if one is affecting a level's matter it will create small flakes of the material that was eliminated, which will then grow and fill in the missing geometry.

Due to the reasons stated prior, it is heavily unadvised for one to drink Bottle 'o Void instances. Stomach acid may fight the Bottle 'o Void at first, though it will result in most, if not all of one's stomach acid to simply vanish, along with the parietal cells which create it. Causing inability to eat and drink food, which will result in death via malnutrition or dehydration.

Though, if one consumes small portions of Bottle 'o Void liquid with Almond Water, it will produce psychoactive effects onto the consumer. These psychoactive effects have been observed to produce a wide arrange of hallucinations commonly related to the childhood memories of the affected person. This feeling will cease within five minutes of consumption, though it proves to be an effective method of tending to wanderers suffering from the Wretched Cycle or depression.
Object 92.lead: Bottle 'o Void is a relatively uncommon substance commonly found in small bottles throughout the Backrooms.

Object 13.title: Object 13
Object 13.description:
Possibly the most powerful objects, Luck Clovers are rare items present within the Backrooms, assigned the number 13. This designation has often been called 'ironic,' due to the object being vastly different than what the number 13 often symbolizes in many cultures. While thirteen is the so-called 'unlucky number,' all instances of Object 13 makes its possessor luckier. The database has been vandalized before to replace its numerical designation, often replacing it with Retractable Entity Detectors.

Object 13 has garnered popularity due to its immensely powerful abilities, granting its possessor 'divine luck.' While it doesn't provide extreme advantages, it still has numerous benefits; such as the ability to easily save an individual's life. Furthermore, Object 13 is able to adjust and adapt to any individual possessing it, generating a solution for any nearby issues. For example, if one were to get infected with Egremond Syndrome a cure would manifest in vicinity. Although curiously, every solution provides the object's user with high amounts of dopamine, which otherwise would not be expected in certain situations.

This phenomenon has been widely studied, particularly in efforts to find a cure for many diseases. Often, Object 13's solutions are analyzed by research teams from various organizations that then attempt to recreate them using similar chemical compounds. However, this has been deemed increasingly difficult, as a result of every solution disappearing 24 hours after its use. As of current, no experiments have been successful in their goal.

Object 13's appearance comes in the form of a clover with emerald and golden borders, made out of jewels. These clovers come in the form of three leaves, and leaf is shaped like a heart. The clovers' dimensions are small to the size of pebbles. Furthermore, the shape of Object 13 varies, sometimes having crystal-like leaves, and other times flat leaves; the color of the object also varies.

Object 15.title: Object 15
Object 15.description:
I - Overview

Object 15, also known as “Firesalt” or “Cinder Crystal”, is a perpetually glowing anomalous crystal found mostly within cave-like or dilapidated levels, which can prove to be a valuable resource if used effectively. However, due to Firesalt constantly remaining at a temperature of approximately 500°C, as well as it being extremely flammable and volatile, handling it carries a plethora of risk and dangers.

Being extremely brittle, Firesalt is quite vulnerable to cracking due to blunt damage, which produces and unique reaction. When broken, a small spark of heat is produced at the area of impact, creating a chain reaction which results in the Firesalt crystal violently exploding, with most small crystals producing a blast force equivalent to that of a hand grenade.

II - Uses

Due to its combustive properties, Firesalt is an extremely effective weapon against Smilers, and the majority of other entities. Additionally, due to its heat-based ectoentropic properties, Firesalt can also be used as a source of heat within a cold levels and as a method of quickly boiling food.

Furthermore, Firesalt has also been historically used to create a massive amount of other anomalous items, listed below:

Firesalt Flashlight

The Firesalt Flashlight is a device which uses the constant bright light Firesalt gives off in order to make a flashlight which lacks the need for any external power source to operate. In order to prevent damage to the flashlight due to the heat of Firesalt, the crystal itself is contained within a metal container with one piece of glass allowing light to escape the flashlight.

Firesteel

Firesteel is an anomalous alloy composed of Firesalt and Iron, made by grinding up Firesalt crystals into a fine dust and putting them into molten metal and mixing the two, resulting in the creation of Firesteel when the mixture finally cools down and solidifies.

Although Firesteel shares the same ectoentropic properties as Firesalt, it is much more durable, making it an ideal choice as a material to use in various weapons, often including swords, knives, and even special bullets made out of Firesteel.

> Open Credits

> Close Credits

Author/Wikitexter: Stormy

Object 30.title: Object 30
Object 30.description:
Object 30, colloquially known as "Retractable Entity Detectors," or simply abbreviated as "R.E.D.s," are very rare objects in the Backrooms. R.E.D. appears as plastic, retractable tube-like structures with LEDs inside. The top, non-retractable part of the device has a white plate on its uppermost point; the top also contains a darkened button, which changes the R.E.D.'s mode. Despite their acronym, R.E.D.s are actually yellow-colored. On the lower part of the device is a hole that allows for one to store small objects inside the device. This hole proves that R.E.D.s are hollow; the LEDs in them are seen from the hole. When pressing the dark button previously mentioned, the R.E.D. will change modes; it has three modes. The first is "Quick Twinkle."  In this mode, the R.E.D.'s LEDs turn on and off rapidly. The second mode is "Slow Twinkle."  In this mode, the R.E.D.'s LEDs turn on and off more slowly. The third mode is "Perpetual Light." In this mode, the LEDs only turn off manually. Despite its name, the R.E.D.'s usage shifted with time; originally, the device's purpose became analyzing entities. Scientists renovated R.E.D.s and created a remote application that analyzes the entity detected by the device. Its LED lights were given a new function: a ray that scans the entity's information, such as vitals. The information is sent to the application and has helped researchers and biologists learn more about entities.

The original usage of the device was more simple: the LEDs would detect an entity, and once an entity was detected, the R.E.D. would begin glowing depending on its current mode. The LEDs would detect the entities by analyzing and comparing a human and another life form's information, distinguishing the human from the entity. This older variation of the device, named "R.E.D. H.20," can be found on levels, unlike the modern device, named "R.E.D. L.V.L.," which is obscure and kept secret. H.20 appears to be smaller than L.V.L., as it has fewer features and its LEDs only execute one task. H.20 stands for "Heap 20," inspired by the device's inventor's surname. Meanwhile, L.V.L. formerly stood for "Leap Volume Loud." The name was given as a joke by one of the projectors due to the loud noise that was produced while and after scanning an entity. Currently, L.V.L. stands for "Learn Validation of Life," due to the massive scientific discoveries due to L.V.L. and the validation of the lives of the entities, as entities are not murdered or injured during R.E.D. L.V.L.'s scan.

It is believed that R.E.D.s have a toy-like appearance to trick less intelligent, irrational entities, as they will appear more friendly and unaggressive. If the device had a more modern appearance, entities would know to destroy it. In order to convince entities better, a sticker taken from a similar, non-detecting item in the Frontrooms was put into the devices. This technique is exceedingly useful for irrational entities; however, intelligent entities can easily find out that the item is a R.E.D. Due to this, researchers developed a design for explorers when dealing with more intelligent entities. This design, known as "H.20 W Int," appears as a robust recreation of a metal bottle, with the top part appearing as a cap and the hole for storage being unexistent. This design is useful for newly discovered entities and levels. Although several of the entities that have been detected by the device have been seen to realize the trick, due to this, new designs are created monthly to continue the research.

During the Flicker, R.E.D.s became an extremely desired item, and wanderers and groups were desperately and mindlessly procuring these devices. Those who didn't have the devices were much more likely to die. R.E.D.s were overvalued and very expensive due to their rarity and utility, making it very difficult to gain them. The devices would be sold and donated to small, yet uncommon, tents in small quantities, randomly scattered around the Backrooms. These tents have disappeared, and their locations were vandalized and destroyed. R.E.D.s were given on first aid kits and at donation centers. It was on the Flicker when R.E.D. L.V.L. and R.E.D. H.20 W Int were first invented, so people could learn new ways to defeat entities and research them independently of intelligence. The data collected from the remote L.V.L. application was well-used to research and protect wanderers during the Flicker, and to this day, the information is still used by hundreds of people who use this data weekly for research.

In 2021, scientists responsible for R.E.D. L.V.L. and H.20 W Int attempted to create artificial intelligence, which would be put into a new R.E.D. A.I.R., which stands for "Artificial Intelligence Runner." A.I.R. appears as a retractable red cube with a foldable screen. This artificial intelligence was an enormous failure, which caused a myriad of wanderers to murder and commit various mistakes. The inventors decided to remove and destroy all instances of R.E.D. A.I.R., and any remaining hidden A.I.R. instances and software were deactivated permanently. Several months later, developers recreated A.I.R. with more data, and some instances could have a moving body. These instances quickly drifted from their original purpose and were trained to destroy everything. It is believed that the developers wanted to attack groups with these A.I.R.s. Groups quickly saw what was happening and began planning to capture these developers. As of now, the instances were dismantled, but the developers were never captured or encountered.

Object 93.title: Object 93
Object 93.description:
Paper candy is a substance described as a collection of paper strips that resemble ribbon paper or come in various unusual colors. They are often found on tables or in fruit bowls in a peculiar manner. While these objects may look like regular paper scraps, they are significantly heavier than typical paper pieces and closer in mass to small candies.

Paper Candy, as candy-like object, typically features a blend of fruit flavors reminiscent of wood pulp and comes in various colors ranging from red to purple. Recent research indicates that Paper Candy may exhibit colors imperceptible to the human eye on its surface. Moreover, specific experiments have demonstrated heightened color sensitivity to Paper Candy among many animals and certain intelligent entities such as Facelings and Pranksters. It is important to note that Paper Candy of the same color often offers different flavors, which can pose a challenge in selecting one that matches personal preference.

The taste of Paper Candy that has been documented so far is as follows:

Level 948.title: Level 948
Level 948.description: Level 948, commonly known as "Road Convenience", is an area that represents an underground tunnel that serves as a passageway to cross a road. This level is notable for its presence of Object 16.
Level 948.lead:
This page looks significantly better in dark mode.
This page looks significantly better in light mode.

Level 948 is the 949th level of the Backrooms. It is seemingly infinite, and it contains a useful and unique object.

exits.text: Noclipping into an air vent leads to Level 34.
Noclipping into the pipes on the ceiling leads to Level 2.
Following an exit sign written "YELLOW" leads one to Level 0.
Following an exit sign written "HUB" leads one to The Hub.
Following an exit sign written "STATION154" leads one to Level 154.
Following an exit sign written "BRIGHTENESS" leads one to Level 228.

Object 25.title: Object 25
Object 25.description:
Object 25, also known as "Flashies", are electronic devices found in different levels, resembling green tactical flashlights from The Frontrooms. On average, they weigh about 120 grams and can range in length from 7 cm to 35 cm; those under 10 cm are known as "miniflashies", while those 10 cm or longer are called "maxiflashies". Miniflashies are less effective, but they are more portable than maxiflashies.

These gadgets can be rarely located in levels with a high amount of cul-de-sacs and turns. In those levels, flashies are found lying on the floor or in closed containers, such as cardboard boxes or wooden drawers. Flashies are often overlooked by wanderers as they can be mistaken for normal flashlights. The only way to distinguish them is their unnaturally perfect cylindrical shape.
Object 25.lead: This page looks significantly better in dark mode.

Object 36.title: Object 36
Object 36.description:
Object 36, also known as "Slime Organ" (singular and plural Slime Organ), is a relatively common object in the Backrooms. It is a slime-like, gelatinous, organic substance of varying colors that grows on corners of indoor levels. Slime Organ take their name from their slimy composition and organ-like appearance and functioning. Despite being very similar to the ordinary slime, Slime Organ detains several noticeable differences to an average slime. Among these differences are: Slime Organ are more watery than the average slime; Slime Organ contain advanced and complicated systems very different from a slime; they are much more resistant; and they are fully organic and edible.

There are a number of variations of Slime Organ, from a Slime Organ that is simply blue to a neon Slime Organ to a cotton candy-smelling Slime Organ.

The interior of a Slime Organ's body is fairly complicated. The entire object is filled with a liquid that feeds the Slime Organ, helps it grow, and assists in generating new cells. This liquid is essential for the Slime Organ and it will quickly rot away if it is not present. Strangely enough, heating a Slime Organ to temperatures higher than 40° Celsius or putting a lot of pressure into it will cause the cells to begin an emergency operation; the cells will begin modifying the Slime Organ's inner liquid and turning it into the same outer substance of the Slime Organ. This makes eating an undecooked or raw Slime Organ an extremely hazardous act, as the Slime Organ may become stuck to the wanderer's throat and begin growing and feeding on the wanderer's meat.

Slime Organ contains a strange property that causes it to extremely quickly regenerate once it is cut in half. This is caused by the excessive amount of cells and proteins within Slime Organ; these cells are quickly deployed to the injury and quickly regenerate it. Due to this, some believe Slime Organ are completely solid. No matter how fast a wanderer or a machine attempts to cut the Slime Organ, the only way for one to see the inner liquid is to collect it using a serynge. One would have to remove one of the organ-like structures within Slime Organ for the regeration process to cease.

Inside Slime Organ, there are several organ-like structures, denoted "glossums," inspired by the person who discovered them. These organ-like structures are essential for the Slime Organ's existence and growing cycle, and a Slime Organ without even just one of these structures would collapse. There are four glossums inside of a Slime Organ; the Cellar, responsible for generating cells of most types; the Reloses, responsible for reproduction; the Takers, outer glossums that collect nutrients and food from outside and provide them to the Slime Organ; and the outer substance covering the entirety of the Slime Organ, and preventing the liquid from leaking and glossums from getting out, named after the same object.

Some specimens of Slime Organ contain "bulbs." Bulbs are inflamated areas within the Slime Organ that are filled with bacteria and viruses and contain no liquid within them. Bulbs are caused by the cells of the Slime Organ itself, who redirect all of the hazardous bacteria to a specific area, deemed useless by them, and the bacteria become stuck into the area. Before cooking a Slime Organ, one should immediately search for bulbs, as the bacteria are freed from the bulb when the Slime Organ is cooked, and they will spread around the object. The best way to remove a bulb is by using tools similar to scissors to cut them off. The danger of a bulb depends on its age; older bulbs contain more bacteria than newer ones. Eating a Slime Organ with a bulb, independently of age, is extremely hazardous for a human, as various viruses and bacteria will enter one's body.

Slime Organ can easily catch dust, hair, and other pieces of dirt on it that are quite difficult to remove due to their size. These pieces of dirt may even be absorbed into the Slime Organ, causing the particles to be spread around the object and making the process of removing them much, much more difficult. Therefore, a Slime Organ should be carefully washed and inspected, and any pieces of dirt must be carefully taken out using tools before cooking and consumption. If one eats Slime Organ with pieces of dirt, other than causing diseases, the pieces may block outer heat from the Slime Organ, causing some liquid to remain, and the Slime Organ may continue growing inside of the wanderer.

When compared side by side, one can notice several differences between a natural Slime Organ and an industrialized one, specifically physical. A natural Slime Organ is generally more messy, with strange patterns and deformities that cause it to appear as a more gelatinous rock. The natural Slime Organ also contains several dirt pieces. Meanwhile, the industrialized Slime Organ is smooth and flat; it is clean and cooked and is only encountered inside containers.
Object 36.lead:
Object 36
Slime Organ

SAFETY
Safe if Cooked

RARITY
Common

BIOHAZARD
Low

AREAS:
Forest and indoor, abandoned levels.

Object 29.title: Object 29
Object 29.description:
Object 29, otherwise known as Kalthoff Rifles (/khælθɑf ˈɹ̠aɪfol/; from Nujemen Aurielleno: Fiuscil Kalthof [fju.sil.kal.tof] /Fiuscil Muskcat [fju.sil.mu.skat] (circa 2021)) are cheap, mass produceable firearms from both the M.E.G. and the Republic of Aurielle. Used primarily in the Korthie Casiagen War, they were produced primarily in industrial levels (i.e. Level 92), and are mainly semi-automatic rifles, akin to muskets or The Frontrooms' M1 Garand.

Unlike other weapons used in war, the Kalthoff Rifles are the most reliable - due to being cheap, mass producible, and effective. Whereas other firearms produced by the M.E.G. are expensive, use a lot of resources, and, while effective, often break down due to the quality of materials within The Backrooms.
Object 29.lead:
Kalthoff Rifles

High quality Kalthoff rifles, of Aurielle origin. Circa 2018, these are older Auriellen models.

Extended Almond Water Sub-Item Dossier (.title: Extended Almond Water Sub-Item Dossier (Collaborative)
Extended Almond Water Sub-Item Dossier (.description:
Perhaps the most interesting aspect of Almond Water is its extreme reactivity with nearly anything. Almond Water is known to make new compounds when mixed with a variety of substances both anomalous and non-anomalous, often resulting in products like Greek Fire when mixed with Carpet Fluid, for example.

Due to the thousands of sub-items and variants of Almond Water currently documented, for general convenience, most of the items created using Almond Water have been listed here instead of within individual pages for ease of organization. All Database users are encouraged to add their own entries to this list to educate other wanderers.

Level 149.title: Level 149
Level 149.description:
Notice from the Republic of Aurielle's Department of State
All entrances into Level 149 are to be avoided at all costs per recent events that transpired due to extreme decontamination and entity clearing efforts.

Nevi Aurielleno Onem Quiusen.

Object 69.title: Object 69
Object 69.description:
Object 69, commonly referred to as the Paralysis Glove, is a rare object found across many different levels. The object has the appearance of a glove, with its exterior covered with a fur reminiscent of an animal, its texture is soft yet unnervingly dense, though it is unknown which animal this fur belongs to, as DNA analysis has been unsuccessful. The fur possesses the ability to paralyze a limb or any part of a living organism. Upon contact with the fur, an organism’s nerve signals in the affected area are halted, as it was found that the signals sent by the nerves were ignored by the body part. Although prolonged exposure can extend the effect, it typically lasts anywhere from 30 minutes to 2 hours.

The object can be found inside of boxes, on top of tables, and on the ground. It is primarily encountered in indoor levels, such as Level 4, Level 5, Level 20, Level 21 etc.

Object 45.title: Object 45
Object 45.description:
Dispositive FAX

Description
The FAX machine is an invention created by the A.A.E.G., specifically one of the 4-S, Austin. He invented the FAX machine to disseminate information and help out explorers in the Backrooms. The FAX machine can send anything: photos, video, sound, written messages, and even calls. While the FAX machine processes all this information in a rudimentary way, it is also able to finely tune messages for better dissemination, which is crucial for its security protocols.

All of this is very useful for explorers since the A.A.E.G. has guaranteed that everything sent via the FAX machine is entirely real and not fake, thus preventing misinformation as it is seen with other groups. This makes the FAX machine extremely useful for getting to new levels or just staying alive in dangerous levels. The FAX machine is one of the easiest objects to use for sending messages, while not losing your state of mind and expending zero effort.

The significance of this technology is highly beneficial for explorers because it provides an accurate reading of their current level. The A.A.E.G. has successfully utilized the FAX for exploration purposes several times when investigating levels, like Level 634, just to name one.

The primary limitation of the FAX is that it is quite a rare piece of technology outside the A.A.E.G., since only members of the A.A.E.G. are allowed to operate the device. You would get in major trouble if you had a normal FAX if you weren’t a member of the A.A.E.G.. However, this was only referring to the latest item, the FAX. The versions that preceded the FAX, such as FAX-Maximus, FAX-Prototype, and FAX-Blue, are completely for public use and are still relatively accessible.

Functions

In particular, the FAX allows for the sending of photos, visually documenting each level and providing a visual means to actually show specific environmental features, obstacles, or details that may represent dangerous or safe zones and give other explorers a more precise visual reference. Another feature the device encompasses is the sending of audio messages, which significantly enhances sending short notes or warnings. This could be helpful when particular details must be given and need to be done so without diversion.

Calls are also enabled with the FAX and could be very helpful in real-time communication for the explorers, especially in life-or-death situations or when making their way through more complex levels. The device also enables text messaging, whereby written information-including specific details like coordinates, codes, or instructions on how to proceed at certain areas of the levels-can be shared.

The combination of these functionalities greatly enhances the safety protocols, allowing effective and proper communication between explorers, which in itself is quite important for countering misinformation from other groups and increasing survival rates at most dangerous levels.

----

Versions of the FAX

|-| FAX-prototipe=

The "FAX-Prototype" was conceived by Austin to fulfill an urgent need for efficient communication within the A.A.E.G. organization in the year of 1994. A.A.E.G. at that time needed a machine that could rapidly and efficiently convey important information—messages, images, and voice data. Given the limited technological infrastructure and the urgency to establish internal communication, Austin, a pioneering member of the organization, took the initiative to develop an operational prototype in the absence of formal training in engineering.

Developing the FAX-Prototype was a challenging task - without an understanding of electronic systems or hardware design, Austin stumbled through trial and error, basic electronic components, and readily available consumer technology. The unit used a pressure-sensitive screen that worked with a stylus since capacitive or finger-touch capability was beyond Austin's understanding at that time. The interface was very simplified with few function buttons, such as "Fax Connect", "Mute" or "Power On" and a speaker phone configuration to enable basic audio transmission.

Austin built this prototype from salvaged or re-purposed electronic parts- possibly old digital assistants, early touch-sensitive monitors, and simple microcontrollers. The case was custom-built or taken from available shells, and used stylus input because of the relatively easy application of resistive touch technology.

While rudimentary compared to today's standards, the FAX-Prototype represented a significant step forward in A.A.E.G.'s evolution, representing not merely the genesis of its in-house technological innovation, but Austin's resourcefulness and tenacity despite his lack of experience.

|-| FAX-inepta=

The “FAX‑Inepta” represents the next evolutionary step following the original FAX‑Prototype. Building on the rudimentary design, this second version adopts a more conventional office‑style form factor that closely resembles the early commercial fax machines introduced in the 1970s and early 1980s but this one was created in the year of 1996.

The design includes a sturdy desktop casing, an integrated paper feeder, and a fixed physical keypad with multiple function buttons. Internally, the device incorporates an optical scanning module paired with a digital modem capable of transmitting facsimile data using Group 3 standards, including built‑in image compression and error correction—the same techniques that reduced transmission time to under one minute per page. Encyclopedia Britannica

This version retains the concept of stylus-based input solely for editing or annotating documents before transmission—since capacitive touch-screen technology remains beyond the creator’s initial expertise—but otherwise adopts real-world hardware components such as a small thermal or laser-based printer to reproduce incoming faxes.

Functionally, the "FAX‑Inepta" significantly enhances reliability, speed, and usability, marking a key milestone in the transition from makeshift prototype to a more mature, semi-industrial communication device.

|-| FAX-Blue=

The "FAX-Blue" was the next major step in developing A.A.E.G.'s communications technology that Austin envisioned in the year of 1998. After the FAX-Inepta's shortcomings were realized, the company required a machine that would not only operate more reliably but would also be capable of processing greater amounts of information with greater speed and stability. To meet this demand, Austin designed the FAX-Blue, a machine that deviated from the primitive improvisations of its predecessors and came with a typical matte blue housing that rendered it more professional and recognizable.

The construction of the FAX-Blue was also a challenging undertaking, as Austin's technical expertise in advanced engineering was still not excellent. But by drawing on lessons obtained from his earlier prototypes, he was able to integrate several technological advancements. The model included an automatic document feeder and a thermal or early dot-matrix printer inside the machine, allowing faster and better-readable reproduction of received messages. The numeric keypad was available on the front panel, along with function keys such as Send, Receive, and Speed Dial, and display status indicators for line activity and power. These additions made the machine much more convenient for day-to-day use in A.A.E.G. operations.

Internally, FAX-Blue employed an optical scanner to read documents and a Group-3-capable modem, which offered higher digital transmission speeds and reduced error-correcting capability. Although Austin retained his stylus port to facilitate manual annotation or correction before sending, finger input was not yet there, as capacitive touch technology remained beyond his technical skills.

Far from being flawless, the FAX-Blue was a definite improvement over A.A.E.G.'s earlier communication systems. It indicated the firm's shift away from experimental improvisation towards semi-professional equipment, as well as Austin's perseverance and step-by-step mastery of technological engineering in the absence of any formal training.

|-| FAX-Maximus=

The "FAX-Maximus" was the most ambitious and complicated model of the entire A.A.E.G that was created in the year of 1999. fax family. Designed as the culmination of everything that had preceded it, it brought together the experience of the Prototype, the Inepta, and the Blue into a single highly advanced machine. Unlike its predecessors, which still had the marks of experimentation and limitation, the FAX-Maximus was a full professional-grade machine with state-of-the-art features and a rugged build to be the best communications machine for the company.

The machine featured a solid and roomy case with an integrated automatic feeder capable of handling multiple pages, a high-speed thermal printing mechanism, and an integrated digital display to make settings and transmissions easier than ever before. Its front panel was equipped with a complete numeric keypad, programmable memory slots for speed dialing, and individual buttons for special functions, all of which combined to make the FAX-Maximus the easiest-to-use and most functional model ever produced under Austin's management.

Internally, the product incorporated a high-quality optical scanning system, improved Group-3 digital modem functionality with more efficient compression algorithms, and a strengthened power supply to support around-the-clock operation. It also had more memory to retain several documents, a functionality that enabled it to be very effective for managing high-volume communications in A.A.E.G. In contrast to its predecessors, the FAX-Maximus was a genuine effort at industrial-grade reliability and professional use.

Yet despite all its revolutionary advances, the FAX-Maximus was beset by a deadly design flaw: it was prone to frequent overheating due to inadequate thermal handling in its compact case. Extended operation caused degradation of performance, malfunction, and in some cases, complete system failure. These issues finally prompted A.A.E.G. to discontinue the model, even though it remained the most advanced fax machine Austin had ever made.

The FAX-Maximus was both the high point and the end of A.A.E.G.'s fax development program—a testament to its capabilities and design, but also a testament to the technical hurdles that could not be surmounted in Austin's push for innovation.

|-| FAX-Portable=

in the year of 1999 the "FAX-Portable" was A.A.E.G.'s most ambitious leap in its constant pursuit of innovation—a machine that sought to combine portability with the robust communication strength of its predecessors. While all the predecessors such as the Prototype, Inepta, Blue, and Maximus had all been confined to office rooms and desks, the Portable was Austin's dream to provide fax communication anywhere, anytime.

Wrapped in a small, foldable casing in the shape of a tiny computer, the FAX-Portable was small but mighty. It included a full keyboard for input, an expansive digital display to monitor in real-time, and an onboard system that could handle several forms of transmissions. While earlier machines had been strictly confined to documents, the Portable could transmit photographs, written communication, voice calls, and even basic video clips—a communications revolution for the individual.

Internally, the FAX-Portable had a thin-line power-saving modem, extra memory modules for storing contact information and files, and advanced software featuring organizational tools such as a calendar, a note-taker facility, and currency converter facilities. Its paperless format made it radically different from all its predecessors, symbolizing Austin's transition towards real digital communication rather than mechanical copying.

It had its faults, though, for all its pioneering appearance. Its compact hardware limited processing speed, and while it advertised multimedia transmission, quality was often constrained by the technology of the era. It was nonetheless a symbol of A.A.E.G.'s willingness to go beyond boundaries and envision the fax as not just a tool of the office, but as an appliance for the individual.

The FAX-Portable thus marked a turning point in the history of A.A.E.G.'s fax line. It was not a question of power, but of freedom, representing Austin's vision of a world liberated from a desk-bound communication, but one that could keep pace with its user—nested, flexible, and inventive.

----

|-| Test A1=

Object 45.lead:
Dispositive FAX

Description
The FAX machine is an invention created by the A.A.E.G., specifically one of the 4-S, Austin. He invented the FAX machine to disseminate information and help out explorers in the Backrooms. The FAX machine can send anything: photos, video, sound, written messages, and even calls. While the FAX machine processes all this information in a rudimentary way, it is also able to finely tune messages for better dissemination, which is crucial for its security protocols.

All of this is very useful for explorers since the A.A.E.G. has guaranteed that everything sent via the FAX machine is entirely real and not fake, thus preventing misinformation as it is seen with other groups. This makes the FAX machine extremely useful for getting to new levels or just staying alive in dangerous levels. The FAX machine is one of the easiest objects to use for sending messages, while not losing your state of mind and expending zero effort.

The significance of this technology is highly beneficial for explorers because it provides an accurate reading of their current level. The A.A.E.G. has successfully utilized the FAX for exploration purposes several times when investigating levels, like Level 634, just to name one.

The primary limitation of the FAX is that it is quite a rare piece of technology outside the A.A.E.G., since only members of the A.A.E.G. are allowed to operate the device. You would get in major trouble if you had a normal FAX if you weren’t a member of the A.A.E.G.. However, this was only referring to the latest item, the FAX. The versions that preceded the FAX, such as FAX-Maximus, FAX-Prototype, and FAX-Blue, are completely for public use and are still relatively accessible.

Functions

In particular, the FAX allows for the sending of photos, visually documenting each level and providing a visual means to actually show specific environmental features, obstacles, or details that may represent dangerous or safe zones and give other explorers a more precise visual reference. Another feature the device encompasses is the sending of audio messages, which significantly enhances sending short notes or warnings. This could be helpful when particular details must be given and need to be done so without diversion.

Calls are also enabled with the FAX and could be very helpful in real-time communication for the explorers, especially in life-or-death situations or when making their way through more complex levels. The device also enables text messaging, whereby written information-including specific details like coordinates, codes, or instructions on how to proceed at certain areas of the levels-can be shared.

The combination of these functionalities greatly enhances the safety protocols, allowing effective and proper communication between explorers, which in itself is quite important for countering misinformation from other groups and increasing survival rates at most dangerous levels.

----

Versions of the FAX

|-| FAX-prototipe=

The "FAX-Prototype" was conceived by Austin to fulfill an urgent need for efficient communication within the A.A.E.G. organization in the year of 1994. A.A.E.G. at that time needed a machine that could rapidly and efficiently convey important information—messages, images, and voice data. Given the limited technological infrastructure and the urgency to establish internal communication, Austin, a pioneering member of the organization, took the initiative to develop an operational prototype in the absence of formal training in engineering.

Developing the FAX-Prototype was a challenging task - without an understanding of electronic systems or hardware design, Austin stumbled through trial and error, basic electronic components, and readily available consumer technology. The unit used a pressure-sensitive screen that worked with a stylus since capacitive or finger-touch capability was beyond Austin's understanding at that time. The interface was very simplified with few function buttons, such as "Fax Connect", "Mute" or "Power On" and a speaker phone configuration to enable basic audio transmission.

Austin built this prototype from salvaged or re-purposed electronic parts- possibly old digital assistants, early touch-sensitive monitors, and simple microcontrollers. The case was custom-built or taken from available shells, and used stylus input because of the relatively easy application of resistive touch technology.

While rudimentary compared to today's standards, the FAX-Prototype represented a significant step forward in A.A.E.G.'s evolution, representing not merely the genesis of its in-house technological innovation, but Austin's resourcefulness and tenacity despite his lack of experience.

|-| FAX-inepta=

The “FAX‑Inepta” represents the next evolutionary step following the original FAX‑Prototype. Building on the rudimentary design, this second version adopts a more conventional office‑style form factor that closely resembles the early commercial fax machines introduced in the 1970s and early 1980s but this one was created in the year of 1996.

The design includes a sturdy desktop casing, an integrated paper feeder, and a fixed physical keypad with multiple function buttons. Internally, the device incorporates an optical scanning module paired with a digital modem capable of transmitting facsimile data using Group 3 standards, including built‑in image compression and error correction—the same techniques that reduced transmission time to under one minute per page. Encyclopedia Britannica

This version retains the concept of stylus-based input solely for editing or annotating documents before transmission—since capacitive touch-screen technology remains beyond the creator’s initial expertise—but otherwise adopts real-world hardware components such as a small thermal or laser-based printer to reproduce incoming faxes.

Functionally, the "FAX‑Inepta" significantly enhances reliability, speed, and usability, marking a key milestone in the transition from makeshift prototype to a more mature, semi-industrial communication device.

|-| FAX-Blue=

The "FAX-Blue" was the next major step in developing A.A.E.G.'s communications technology that Austin envisioned in the year of 1998. After the FAX-Inepta's shortcomings were realized, the company required a machine that would not only operate more reliably but would also be capable of processing greater amounts of information with greater speed and stability. To meet this demand, Austin designed the FAX-Blue, a machine that deviated from the primitive improvisations of its predecessors and came with a typical matte blue housing that rendered it more professional and recognizable.

The construction of the FAX-Blue was also a challenging undertaking, as Austin's technical expertise in advanced engineering was still not excellent. But by drawing on lessons obtained from his earlier prototypes, he was able to integrate several technological advancements. The model included an automatic document feeder and a thermal or early dot-matrix printer inside the machine, allowing faster and better-readable reproduction of received messages. The numeric keypad was available on the front panel, along with function keys such as Send, Receive, and Speed Dial, and display status indicators for line activity and power. These additions made the machine much more convenient for day-to-day use in A.A.E.G. operations.

Internally, FAX-Blue employed an optical scanner to read documents and a Group-3-capable modem, which offered higher digital transmission speeds and reduced error-correcting capability. Although Austin retained his stylus port to facilitate manual annotation or correction before sending, finger input was not yet there, as capacitive touch technology remained beyond his technical skills.

Far from being flawless, the FAX-Blue was a definite improvement over A.A.E.G.'s earlier communication systems. It indicated the firm's shift away from experimental improvisation towards semi-professional equipment, as well as Austin's perseverance and step-by-step mastery of technological engineering in the absence of any formal training.

|-| FAX-Maximus=

The "FAX-Maximus" was the most ambitious and complicated model of the entire A.A.E.G that was created in the year of 1999. fax family. Designed as the culmination of everything that had preceded it, it brought together the experience of the Prototype, the Inepta, and the Blue into a single highly advanced machine. Unlike its predecessors, which still had the marks of experimentation and limitation, the FAX-Maximus was a full professional-grade machine with state-of-the-art features and a rugged build to be the best communications machine for the company.

The machine featured a solid and roomy case with an integrated automatic feeder capable of handling multiple pages, a high-speed thermal printing mechanism, and an integrated digital display to make settings and transmissions easier than ever before. Its front panel was equipped with a complete numeric keypad, programmable memory slots for speed dialing, and individual buttons for special functions, all of which combined to make the FAX-Maximus the easiest-to-use and most functional model ever produced under Austin's management.

Internally, the product incorporated a high-quality optical scanning system, improved Group-3 digital modem functionality with more efficient compression algorithms, and a strengthened power supply to support around-the-clock operation. It also had more memory to retain several documents, a functionality that enabled it to be very effective for managing high-volume communications in A.A.E.G. In contrast to its predecessors, the FAX-Maximus was a genuine effort at industrial-grade reliability and professional use.

Yet despite all its revolutionary advances, the FAX-Maximus was beset by a deadly design flaw: it was prone to frequent overheating due to inadequate thermal handling in its compact case. Extended operation caused degradation of performance, malfunction, and in some cases, complete system failure. These issues finally prompted A.A.E.G. to discontinue the model, even though it remained the most advanced fax machine Austin had ever made.

The FAX-Maximus was both the high point and the end of A.A.E.G.'s fax development program—a testament to its capabilities and design, but also a testament to the technical hurdles that could not be surmounted in Austin's push for innovation.

|-| FAX-Portable=

in the year of 1999 the "FAX-Portable" was A.A.E.G.'s most ambitious leap in its constant pursuit of innovation—a machine that sought to combine portability with the robust communication strength of its predecessors. While all the predecessors such as the Prototype, Inepta, Blue, and Maximus had all been confined to office rooms and desks, the Portable was Austin's dream to provide fax communication anywhere, anytime.

Wrapped in a small, foldable casing in the shape of a tiny computer, the FAX-Portable was small but mighty. It included a full keyboard for input, an expansive digital display to monitor in real-time, and an onboard system that could handle several forms of transmissions. While earlier machines had been strictly confined to documents, the Portable could transmit photographs, written communication, voice calls, and even basic video clips—a communications revolution for the individual.

Internally, the FAX-Portable had a thin-line power-saving modem, extra memory modules for storing contact information and files, and advanced software featuring organizational tools such as a calendar, a note-taker facility, and currency converter facilities. Its paperless format made it radically different from all its predecessors, symbolizing Austin's transition towards real digital communication rather than mechanical copying.

It had its faults, though, for all its pioneering appearance. Its compact hardware limited processing speed, and while it advertised multimedia transmission, quality was often constrained by the technology of the era. It was nonetheless a symbol of A.A.E.G.'s willingness to go beyond boundaries and envision the fax as not just a tool of the office, but as an appliance for the individual.

The FAX-Portable thus marked a turning point in the history of A.A.E.G.'s fax line. It was not a question of power, but of freedom, representing Austin's vision of a world liberated from a desk-bound communication, but one that could keep pace with its user—nested, flexible, and inventive.

Object 26.title: Object 26
Object 26.description:
Object 26: Moonlantern

Moonlanterns are advanced types of flashlights that use Glowing Moonstone as a light source. Most of them have 5 beacons, one at the center and the other 2 at the sides. Though sometimes it is possible to find standard flashlight structures with just one. It emits a soft bluish light, and its most known for possessing the unique ability to highlight many environmental clues, such as objects, exits, and sometimes even entities (doesn't apply to dark-based entities).
Usage

Moonlanterns are very useful and are always great to have if you're exploring any levels, especially dark ones. Working in a way similar to Flashies, they can highlight many important items such as Almond Water, Luck Clovers and Moth Jelly. They can also highlight potential exits and entities, making them a good asset when it comes to escaping levels safely and harvesting resources. While this is all good, Moonlanterns cannot highlight entities that thrive in the dark, making the user still susceptible to threats such as Smilers.

Despite more advanced Moonlanterns having additional beacons, this doesn't mean Moonlanterns can be used as a casual light source and should only be used if there are no other alternatives for a light source. This is because of how rare Glowing Moonstones are and how quick they can expire if used significantly. Moonlanterns are very rare to find, but they can be found in big colonies or outposts in some spatial levels, and very rarely inside chests within Luna Villages in Level 700.

A more simple way of making a moonlantern is replacing a flashlight's bulb with Glowing Moonstone, though you'd still have to find it, and as of now, they're only known to be in Level 700.
History

Moonlanterns were discovered when a group of M.E.G. specialists sent for resource extraction found a lamp fueled by Glowing Moonstone inside one of the buildings of a Deserted Village. This lamp was later taken to a M.E.G. base and was studied and replicated. It would at first be only for M.E.G. affiliates until The Flicker, when, due to the abundance of darkness, it became widespread across The Backrooms.

After The Flicker, due to more widespread awareness about the utility of this object, it continued to be used in many advanced groups and colonies.
Disadvantages

While Moonlanterns are very effective, they also have noticeable disadvantages: Having a significantly lower battery duration than average flashlights and a lack of accessibility to Glowing Moonstone, makes them inconvenient to use for long periods of time and costly to make. Additionally, it is important to note Moonlanterns only work when its nighttime on when the lights are off in a level. This means Moonlanterns are instantly obsolete in any level with frequent light. When using Moonlanterns, the best solution is to not completely rely on them to scavenge or escape levels. It is also recommended to mindfully conserve a Moonlantern's power with at least 1-2 uses per level, as you don't know when you could need it the most. Again, due to their low fuel time, unless they're being used for their specific purpose, Moonlanterns are an unreliable common light source, and its best to simply use normal flashlights if you want to use them just to illuminate.

}}

It emits a soft bluish light, and its most known for possessing the unique ability to highlight many environmental clues, such as objects, exits, and sometimes even entities (doesn't apply to dark-based entities).
Usage

Moonlanterns are very useful and are always great to have if you're exploring any levels, especially dark ones. Working in a way similar to Flashies, they can highlight many important items such as Almond Water, Luck Clovers and Moth Jelly. They can also highlight potential exits and entities, making them a good asset when it comes to escaping levels safely and harvesting resources. While this is all good, Moonlanterns cannot highlight entities that thrive in the dark, making the user still susceptible to threats such as Smilers.

Despite more advanced Moonlanterns having additional beacons, this doesn't mean Moonlanterns can be used as a casual light source and should only be used if there are no other alternatives for a light source. This is because of how rare Glowing Moonstones are and how quick they can expire if used significantly. Moonlanterns are very rare to find, but they can be found in big colonies or outposts in some spatial levels, and very rarely inside chests within Luna Villages in Level 700.

A more simple way of making a moonlantern is replacing a flashlight's bulb with Glowing Moonstone, though you'd still have to find it, and as of now, they're only known to be in Level 700.
History

Moonlanterns were discovered when a group of M.E.G. specialists sent for resource extraction found a lamp fueled by Glowing Moonstone inside one of the buildings of a Deserted Village. This lamp was later taken to a M.E.G. base and was studied and replicated. It would at first be only for M.E.G. affiliates until The Flicker, when, due to the abundance of darkness, it became widespread across The Backrooms.

After The Flicker, due to more widespread awareness about the utility of this object, it continued to be used in many advanced groups and colonies.
Disadvantages

While Moonlanterns are very effective, they also have noticeable disadvantages: Having a significantly lower battery duration than average flashlights and a lack of accessibility to Glowing Moonstone, makes them inconvenient to use for long periods of time and costly to make. Additionally, it is important to note Moonlanterns only work when its nighttime on when the lights are off in a level. This means Moonlanterns are instantly obsolete in any level with frequent light. When using Moonlanterns, the best solution is to not completely rely on them to scavenge or escape levels. It is also recommended to mindfully conserve a Moonlantern's power with at least 1-2 uses per level, as you don't know when you could need it the most. Again, due to their low fuel time, unless they're being used for their specific purpose, Moonlanterns are an unreliable common light source, and its best to simply use normal flashlights if you want to use them just to illuminate.

}}

Object 55.title: Object 55
Object 55.description:
Interlevel Hyperlinks (Hyperlinks for short; Links or Gates colloquially) are structures of various sizes and shapes that allow uninterrupted transportation between levels in various ways. There are several different types of Interlevel Hyperlinks that all have their upsides and downsides.

There are 4 types of Hyperlinks:

*Hyperdoors

*Hyperclips

*Exit Hyperlinks

*Voidrail Hyperlinks

Object 100.title: Object 100
Object 100.description:
Object 100 - Supercluster Servers

Object 100, also known as "Supercluster Servers," consists of an undefined set of servers connected to a distributed cloud network of metadata underlying the Back-Net. These servers access information that describes the entirety of the current and diverse source code utilized across Back-Net and are responsible for the constant functioning of this source code

The servers are highly indestructible, tests prove that the structure of the servers appears not to be affected by energy transfer, nullifying joule interactions. Furthermore, it is theorized that servers operate independently of electrical power, being completely autonomous

The servers operate with a performance metric based on an unknown computation surpassing the exascale, capable of performing consecutive calculations for code interpretation. It is theorized that these servers function as "background feeders," autonomously interpreting code through continuous processes that update the Database with new pages and codes being constantly analyzed, allowing every Terminals to operate

Tests using a supercomputer, developed by The Kauer Research Organization and M.E.G., in conjunction with the with the servers’ infrastructure, demonstrated that entire levels could be visualized in a meta-informational state when searched within the cloud. However, most communications between computers and servers result solely in the visualization of these codes, as if the cloud code were blocked from any type of change or modification

It is theorized that the metadata complex within the cloud hosted by these servers is responsible for the anomalous properties of The End's Computer, with the computer serving as a cloud management console interface. Because through the computer it is possible to modify the source code of the metadata contained in the cloud, not only changing the structure of the modified page in the Back-net, but also the level or subject to which the page is related

This metadata is believed to describe not only the data on the Back-net but also their real-world counterparts. It acts as a superior informational metadata layer, wherein both the page of a level is viewed as code, and the level itself in reality is also subject to this coding structure being processed. With the servers served not just as background feeders for Back-net, but for reality itself
